title: BreakfastFlow: Managing Hotel Breakfast Demand
shortDescription: A QR-based booking system that helps hotel guests avoid crowded breakfast hours using real-time availability and flexible booking.
introduction: Based on my experience working in gastronomy and hotel environments, I observed how peak hours often become overcrowded, leading to long wait times, overwhelmed staff, and a poor guest experience.
problemStatement: Guests often arrive at hotel breakfast at the same time due to a lack of visibility into occupancy, resulting in overcrowding, long waits, and frustration. At the same time, staff lack tools to manage demand and distribute guest flow effectively.

processNarrative.description: Help guests choose less crowded breakfast times to reduce congestion, while giving staff clear insight into demand and keeping the experience flexible.

I designed a QR-based booking system that reduces breakfast congestion by guiding guests toward less crowded time slots through real-time occupancy insights. Guests can explore availability, see peak times, and optionally reserve a slot while retaining the flexibility to arrive without booking.

To support decision-making, the system uses clear visual states ({{green:quiet}}, {{orange:steady}}, {{red:full}}) and automatically validates capacity to prevent overcrowding.

**Core concept:** Influence behavior without enforcing it.

Guests access the system via QR code, view breakfast availability, and select a preferred time slot. The system validates availability in real time and suggests alternatives if a slot is unavailable. After booking, guests can manage their reservation by modifying or canceling it as needed.

Time slots that are in the past or already full are shown but cannot be selected. Slot availability is checked in real time before a booking is confirmed. When a selected slot is no longer available, the system recommends another option. Guests can choose between reserving in advance or arriving more flexibly

Translated a real-world service problem into a clear and structured digital solution. Shifted from screen-level thinking to system-level design, focusing on flows, rules, and interactions. Used simple visual signals to improve clarity and support user decision-making. Designed for both user needs and operational constraints, balancing flexibility with control. Reduced complexity by prioritizing essential features and keeping the experience intuitive

metrics: **Visible availability states** make occupancy easy to understand
**Disabled unavailable slots** reduce mistakes while preserving clarity
**Alternative suggestions** keep users moving forward instead of interrupting the flow
**A lightweight booking flow** makes the experience quick and easy to use
**Flexible booking behavior** balances guest choice with operational needs

title: A Smarter Shift Management System
shortDescription: Coordinating staff schedules while allowing flexible shift swaps with manager approval.
introduction: While working in gastronomy and hotel environments, I experienced firsthand how shift coordination often relies on manual communication, making it difficult to manage swaps, preferences, and last-minute changes.
problemStatement: Hotel teams lack a simple way to manage shift swaps and schedule changes, leading to miscommunication, understaffed shifts, and frustration for both staff and managers.

processNarrative.description: Design a flexible system that enables staff to easily view, manage, and swap shifts while ensuring reliable coverage for managers.

A digital shift management system that simplifies scheduling by allowing staff to view shifts, request swaps, and set availability.

Managers can approve changes and maintain proper staffing through automated validation, reducing miscommunication and manual coordination.

Staff view their schedule → request a shift swap → system checks availability and coverage → manager approves (if needed) → schedule updates in real time. If a swap would cause understaffing, the system prevents it or suggests alternatives.
Created low and mid-fidelity wireframes to validate layout and interaction patterns before moving to visual design.

Shift swaps are validated against staffing requirements to ensure adequate coverage. The system automatically detects and prevents scheduling conflicts. Staff preferences and availability inform shift assignments. Managers can review and override requests when necessary. Confirmed changes are reflected instantly across the schedule

Designed for multiple user roles, balancing staff needs with managerial control. Developed a stronger understanding of constraints, validation rules, and edge cases. Simplified complex operational logic into clear and intuitive user flows. Used system feedback to support user decisions and reduce confusion. Balanced flexibility for users with the need for reliable and consistent operations.

metrics: Rule-based interactions ensure only valid shift changes are allowed
Transparent system feedback helps users understand outcomes and next steps
Role-based workflows support both staff actions and managerial control
Visible conflicts make staffing gaps and high-demand shifts easy to identify
Streamlined interactions reduce complexity and manual coordination
